cards.text: To include std::array in your program, add the header {{c1::<array>}}
To declare a std::array of 5 ints, write {{c1::std::array<int, 5> arr;}} where the first template parameter is the {{c2::element type}} and the second is the {{c3::array length}}

cards.front: What are the two categories of arrays in C++?
cards.back: Fixed-size arrays (length known at instantiation, cannot be changed) and dynamic arrays (can be resized at runtime)

cards.front: What is the primary advantage of std::array over std::vector?
cards.back: std::array supports constexpr in most contexts, allowing for compile-time optimization and more robust code. std::vector only supports constexpr in very limited contexts.

cards.text: To initialize a std::array with explicit values, use aggregate initialization: {{c1::std::array<int, 5> arr { 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 };}}

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: The array length template parameter is missing. std::array requires both the element type and array length.
cards.explanation: Unlike std::vector, std::array always requires you to specify the length as a template parameter: std::array<int, 5>

cards.text: To initialize a std::array to all zeros, use {{c1::std::array<int, 5> arr {};}} with empty braces

cards.front: What happens if you provide fewer initializers than the array length in std::array?
cards.back: The remaining elements are value-initialized (initialized to zero for fundamental types). For example, std::array<int, 5> arr { 1, 2 }; results in { 1, 2, 0, 0, 0 }

cards.text: In C++17 and later, you can use CTAD to omit the template parameters: {{c1::std::array arr { 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 };}} and the compiler deduces the type

cards.front: Can you assign one std::array to another using the assignment operator?
cards.back: Yes, std::array supports assignment. Both arrays must have the same element type and length. Example: arr1 = arr2;

cards.concept: When to use std::array vs std::vector
cards.explanation:
Use std::array when:
  • The array length is known at compile time and won't change
  • You need constexpr support
  • You want maximum performance
Use std::vector when you need dynamic resizing or the size is determined at runtime.
examples: std::vector<int> scores;      // Dynamic: user input determines size

cards.text: To create a compile-time constant array, declare it as {{c1::constexpr std::array<int, 5> arr { 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 };}}
